---
title: "Running it in Docker"
---

A tool that asks you to trust it with confidential documents should be easy to inspect and easy to try.
The image is one statically linked binary on `distroless/static`, about 19 MB.
There is no shell in it, no package manager and no interpreter, so there is very little to read and very little to go wrong.

The default build does not depend on an HTTP client at all, since that sits behind the `ner` feature.
The image therefore has no capacity to make a network request, whatever it is asked to do.

```bash
docker pull ghcr.io/mcanouil/oboro:latest
```

## The vault has to outlive the container

::: {.callout-important}

## Mount a volume, or the document cannot be restored

A container's filesystem disappears when the container exits, and the mapping between placeholders and real values lives in that filesystem.

Without `-v oboro-vault:/vault`, `clean` will happily produce a sanitised document whose values are gone the moment the command finishes.
`restore` then leaves every placeholder untouched and warns that it does not recognise them, which is the last chance you get to notice.

:::

Create the volume once:

```bash
docker volume create oboro-vault
```

Then mount it on every run.
The image sets `OBORO_VAULT` and `OBORO_KEY_FILE` to point inside it, so no command has to repeat the paths.

## Cleaning a document

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -v oboro-vault:/vault \
  -v "$PWD":/work -w /work \
  --user "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
  ghcr.io/mcanouil/oboro:latest clean contract.docx
```

`--user` matters: without it the container writes as its own unprivileged user and `contract.clean.md` arrives on your disk owned by someone else.

## Restoring an answer

The same volume, so the mapping is there:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -v oboro-vault:/vault \
  -v "$PWD":/work -w /work \
  --user "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
  ghcr.io/mcanouil/oboro:latest restore answer.md
```

## Reviewing

`review` is a terminal interface, so it needs a terminal attached:

```bash
docker run --rm -it \
  -v oboro-vault:/vault \
  -v "$PWD":/work -w /work \
  --user "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
  ghcr.io/mcanouil/oboro:latest review contract.docx
```

## Worth a shell function

The flags are the same every time, which is exactly what a shell function is for:

```bash
oboro() {
  docker run --rm -it \
    -v oboro-vault:/vault \
    -v "$PWD":/work -w /work \
    --user "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
    ghcr.io/mcanouil/oboro:latest "$@"
}
```

After that, `oboro clean contract.docx` behaves as though it were installed.

## What the image cannot do

Optical character recognition and the recognition model both need system libraries, and the model needs a 348 MB download.
Putting either in the image would cost every user the weight of a capability most of them will not enable, so neither is included.

The image therefore reads `.txt`, `.md`, `.docx`, `.xlsx` and text-based `.pdf`, and finds structured values along with anything on your denylist.
Names it has not been told about need a build with `--features ner`, described in the [quickstart](quickstart.qmd).

Run `doctor` to see what any given build can do:

```bash
docker run --rm -v oboro-vault:/vault ghcr.io/mcanouil/oboro:latest doctor
```

## Building it yourself

The `Dockerfile` is in the repository root and is short enough to read in a minute:

```bash
docker build -t oboro .
```
